title: Random Variables & Expected Value
blurb: Turning outcomes into numbers: probability distributions, expected value, variance, and what makes a game fair.
buildsOn: Units 5–6 — distributions are probability models wearing numbers.

lessons.title: Random Variables & Their Distributions
lessons.concreteComparison: Flip two coins and count the heads. The experiment's outcomes are HH, HT, TH, TT — but what you WROTE DOWN was a number: $2, 1, 1, 0$. That number-with-a-random-value is a **random variable**, and its complete personality fits in a tiny table: $P(0) = \frac{1}{4}$, $P(1) = \frac{1}{2}$, $P(2) = \frac{1}{4}$. Once chance wears numbers, you can do arithmetic on luck.
lessons.objective: Define random variables, build their probability distributions from sample spaces, and verify distributions sum to 1.
concept: A **random variable** $X$ assigns a NUMBER to every outcome of an experiment: heads counted, sum rolled, defectives drawn, money won. Same experiment, different questions → different random variables.

Its **probability distribution** is the table of each possible value with its probability — built by collecting the outcomes that map to each value: $X = 1$ collects HT and TH, so $P(X{=}1) = \frac{2}{4}$.
The audit never changes: every probability in $[0, 1]$, and the column sums to EXACTLY $1$. A distribution that sums to $0.95$ has a missing value or a counting slip.
Cumulative questions read off the same table: $P(X \ge 1) = P(1) + P(2) = \frac{3}{4}$ — or complement: $1 - P(0)$. Everything from Units 5–6 applies; the events just have numeric names now.
lessons.keyIdea: A random variable numbers the outcomes; its distribution table (values + probabilities summing to 1) is its complete description.

facts.title: The distribution
facts.explanation: All values' probabilities exhaust the experiment — the audit that catches missing cases.

facts.title: Collecting outcomes
facts.explanation: Each value's probability gathers every outcome that maps to it.

workedExamples.statement: Two coins are flipped; $X = $ number of heads. Build the distribution of $X$.
workedExamples.solution: Outcomes: HH, HT, TH, TT (equally likely). $X = 0$: TT → $\frac{1}{4}$. $X = 1$: HT, TH → $\frac{2}{4}$. $X = 2$: HH → $\frac{1}{4}$. Audit: $\frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{4} + \frac{1}{4} = 1$ ✓.

workedExamples.statement: Two dice are rolled; $X = $ the sum. Find $P(X = 7)$, $P(X = 2)$, and $P(X \ge 10)$.
A distribution is proposed: $P(X{=}1) = 0.2$, $P(X{=}2) = 0.5$, $P(X{=}3) = 0.4$. Diagnose it.
workedExamples.solution: Sum: $0.2 + 0.5 + 0.4 = 1.1 > 1$ — not a distribution. Something is double-counted (or a probability is wrong). The sum-to-one audit rejects it before any further work.

commonMistakes.text: Listing the VALUES as if equally likely.
commonMistakes.correction: X = heads in two flips takes values 0, 1, 2 — but they're NOT 1/3 each. Probabilities come from counting outcomes per value: 1/4, 1/2, 1/4.
It takes five seconds and catches missing values, double counts, and arithmetic slips. No distribution leaves the desk unsummed.

tryIt.statement: One die is rolled; $X = 1$ if the roll is a six, else $X = 0$. Build the distribution.
tryIt.solution: $P(X{=}1) = \frac{1}{6}$, $P(X{=}0) = \frac{5}{6}$. Sum $= 1$ ✓. (This tiny two-value variable is called a Bernoulli trial — Unit 8's atom.)

tryIt.statement: Three coins; $X = $ number of heads. Find $P(X \ge 2)$.

steps.title: Chance wears numbers
beats: Two coins: outcomes HH, HT, TH, TT.
You wrote down: $2, 1, 1, 0$ — the HEAD COUNT.
A random variable = a number attached to every outcome.
Numbers unlock arithmetic on luck: averages, spreads, prices.

steps.title: The distribution is the personality
beats: List each possible VALUE.
Collect the outcomes mapping to it; that's its probability.
Audit: sums to exactly $1$. Every time. No exceptions.

steps.title: X = heads in two flips
steps.teach: Flip twice and count heads: $X$ lands on 0, 1, or 2. The bars show the whole distribution at a glance — the middle bar is twice as tall because two outcomes (HT and TH) both give one head. Tap a bar to read its probability. Heights always add to 1: the distribution accounts for everything that can happen.

steps.title: Why is 1 the favorite?
steps.prompt: For two coins, why is $P(X{=}1) = \frac{1}{2}$ while $P(X{=}0) = \frac{1}{4}$?
options: Two outcomes (HT, TH) map to 1; only TT maps to 0
It isn't — all three values are $\frac{1}{3}$
steps.explanation: Probabilities count the OUTCOMES behind each value: 1 collects two of the four; 0 collects one. "Middle values likelier" happens often (more ways to balance) but the reason is always the outcome count — never a law you can skip checking.

steps.title: At least, again
steps.explanation: Complement reflex: $1 - P(X{=}0) = 1 - \frac{1}{8} = \frac{7}{8}$. Unit 5's move, now in random-variable clothes.

steps.title: Capital X, little x
steps.body: Capital $X$ names the random variable (the machine); lowercase $x$ names a particular value (an output). $P(X = x)$ reads "the probability the machine outputs $x$". Sloppy case leads to sloppy thinking — keep the machine and its outputs distinct.

steps.title: Insurance tables predate the theory
steps.body: Edmond Halley (of comet fame) built life-expectancy tables for the city of Breslau in 1693 — distribution tables of age-at-death, used to price annuities — decades before random variables were formalized. Business needed the table before mathematics named it.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Random variables number the outcomes; distributions tabulate value ↦ probability.
Collect outcomes per value; audit the sum to 1 always.
Cumulative events use the same table — complements included.

lessons.title: Expected Value
lessons.concreteComparison: A raffle sells $100$ tickets at $1000₮$ each; one wins $50{,}000₮$. Is a ticket worth it? Your winnings-per-ticket, averaged over ALL tickets: $\frac{50000}{100} = 500₮$ — you pay $1000₮$ for something worth $500₮$ on average. That long-run per-play average is the **expected value**, and it's the single most useful number a distribution owns.
lessons.objective: Compute E(X) = Σ x·P(x), interpret it as the long-run average, and locate it as the distribution's balance point.
concept: The **expected value** weighs each value by its probability: $$E(X) = \sum_x x \cdot P(X = x)$$ — the long-run average of the variable over many repetitions of the experiment.
It is NOT the most likely value and often isn't even a possible one: a die's expectation is $E(X) = \frac{1+2+3+4+5+6}{6} = 3.5$ — a value no face shows. "Expected" means average, not anticipated.
Physically, $E(X)$ is the distribution's BALANCE POINT: put the probabilities as weights on a number line and $E(X)$ is where it balances. Skewed weights drag the balance toward the heavy tail.

Interpretation discipline: $E(X) = 500₮$ per raffle ticket means that across thousands of tickets the average payout settles near $500₮$ — single plays wobble freely. It's Unit 5's long-run promise with numbers attached.
lessons.keyIdea: E(X) = Σ x·P(x): probability-weighted average = long-run per-play value = the distribution's balance point.

facts.title: Expected value
facts.explanation: Each value weighted by its probability — the long-run average.

facts.title: Not a prediction
facts.explanation: The average need not be a possible value; 'expected' means mean, not anticipated.

workedExamples.statement: Compute the expected value of one fair die roll.
workedExamples.solution: $E(X) = \frac{1}{6}(1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 6) = \frac{21}{6} = 3.5$. No face shows $3.5$ — it's the long-run average of many rolls, not a forecast of one.

workedExamples.statement: The raffle: $100$ tickets, one $50{,}000₮$ prize. Find the expected winnings per $1000₮$ ticket, and the expected NET.
workedExamples.solution: $E(\text{winnings}) = 50000 \times \frac{1}{100} + 0 \times \frac{99}{100} = 500₮$. Net of the ticket price: $500 - 1000 = -500₮$ per play. Buyers donate, on average, half the ticket price.

workedExamples.statement: $X = $ number of heads in two coin flips. Find $E(X)$ from the distribution.
workedExamples.solution: $E(X) = 0 \cdot \frac{1}{4} + 1 \cdot \frac{1}{2} + 2 \cdot \frac{1}{4} = 1$. Balance check: the distribution is symmetric around $1$ — the balance point confirms it.

commonMistakes.text: Averaging the values without the weights.
commonMistakes.correction: (0 + 1 + 2)/3 = 1 happens to work for symmetric coins but fails in general: the raffle's (0 + 50000)/2 = 25000₮ is fantasy. Weights are the whole point: Σ x·P(x).
E = 3.5 doesn't predict any roll, and E = −500₮ doesn't mean each ticket loses exactly 500₮. It's the long-run per-play average — singles wobble.

tryIt.statement: A spinner pays $0₮$ with probability $\frac{1}{2}$, $200₮$ with probability $\frac{1}{3}$, and $600₮$ with probability $\frac{1}{6}$. Find $E(\text{payout})$.

steps.title: What is a raffle ticket worth?
beats: Average winnings per ticket: $\frac{50000}{100} = 500₮$.
You pay $1000₮$ for an average of $500₮$.
That per-play average is the EXPECTED VALUE — the price tag of chance.

steps.title: Weigh, then add
beats: $E(X) = \sum x \cdot P(x)$ — every value times its weight.
Die: $\frac{1+2+\cdots+6}{6} = 3.5$. Not a possible roll!
"Expected" = long-run average, NOT the likeliest outcome.
Picture: probabilities as weights on a ruler — $E(X)$ is the balance point.

steps.title: The mean is where it balances
steps.teach: A spinner pays 1, 2, or 5 points with probabilities 0.5, 0.3, 0.2. Tap each bar to see its contribution $x \cdot P(x)$; the triangle marks their sum, $E[X] = 0.5 + 0.6 + 1.0 = 2.1$. Notice the balance point isn't any value the spinner can actually land on — the expected value is a **long-run average**, not a prediction of one spin.

steps.title: Weighted, not naive
steps.prompt: $X$ pays $10$ with probability $0.9$ and $100$ with probability $0.1$. $E(X) = $ ?
steps.explanation: $10(0.9) + 100(0.1) = 9 + 10 = 19$. The $55$ is the unweighted midpoint — ignoring that $100$ is rare; $10$ ignores the jackpot entirely.

steps.title: What −500₮ means
steps.prompt: The raffle's expected NET is $-500₮$ per ticket. Which reading is right?
options: Over many tickets, losses average about 500₮ each
steps.explanation: Expectation speaks about the long-run average. Individual tickets lose $1000₮$ (the 99%) or gain $49{,}000₮$ (the lucky 1%); NO ticket loses 500₮. The average is a center, not an experience.

steps.title: Rare jackpots drag averages
steps.body: A tiny probability on a huge value moves E(X) far above the typical outcome — most lottery players do worse than the (already negative) expectation suggests "feels" typical. When a distribution is skewed, report E(X) AND the most likely outcome; they tell different truths.

steps.title: The concept is older than the word 'probability'
steps.body: Pascal and Fermat's 1654 letters solved the "problem of points" — how to split a pot when a game stops early — by computing what each player could EXPECT from the unplayed rounds. Expected value came first; probability was formalized to serve it. The money question built the mathematics.

steps.title: What you learned
points: E(X) = Σ x·P(x) — the probability-weighted average.
It's the long-run per-play value and the balance point; often not a possible value.
Skewed distributions: expectation and typical outcome diverge — report with care.

lessons.title: Fair Games & the House Edge
lessons.concreteComparison: A carnival dice game: pay $500₮$, roll one die, win $100₮$ times the roll. Average winnings: $100 \times 3.5 = 350₮$ — the stall pockets $150₮$ per play, every play, forever. What entry fee WOULD make it fair? Exactly $350₮$: the expected winnings. A game is **fair** when the price equals the expectation — and almost nothing you can pay to play is.
lessons.objective: Price games with expected value: compute expected net, define fairness, and quantify the house edge.
concept: A game is **fair** when the expected NET is zero — entry fee equals expected winnings: $E(\text{net}) = E(\text{winnings}) - \text{fee} = 0$. Fair games neither enrich nor drain either side in the long run.

The **house edge** is the negative of the player's expected net, often expressed per unit staked: the carnival's $150₮$ on a $500₮$ fee is a $30\%$ edge — brutal by casino standards ($1$–$5\%$), generous by lottery standards ($\approx 50\%$).
Two equivalent framings — get comfortable with both: expected NET (fold the fee into the values as losses/gains) or expected WINNINGS minus fee. Same answer, different bookkeeping.

Fairness math also SETS prizes: to make a fee $F$ fair with win-probability $p$, the prize must be $\frac{F}{p}$. A fair $1$-in-$100$ raffle at $1000₮$ per ticket needs a $100{,}000₮$ prize — real raffles offering half that are running a $50\%$ edge.
lessons.keyIdea: Fair means E(net) = 0 — fee equals expected winnings; the shortfall is the house edge, and it compounds forever.

facts.title: Fairness
facts.explanation: The price of a fair game is exactly its expected payout.

facts.title: Fair prize
facts.explanation: Win probability p at fee F is fair only when the prize refunds F/p.

workedExamples.statement: Carnival game: pay $500₮$, roll a die, win $100₮ \times$ roll. Find the player's expected net and the fair fee.
workedExamples.solution: $E(\text{winnings}) = 100 \times E(\text{roll}) = 100 \times 3.5 = 350₮$. Net: $350 - 500 = -150₮$ per play. Fair fee: $350₮$.

workedExamples.statement: A wheel has $10$ equal sectors: one pays $2000₮$, two pay $500₮$, the rest pay nothing. What entry fee makes the game fair?
A bet pays even money ($+x$ or $-x$) on "at least one six in two dice". Using $P = \frac{11}{36}$, find the player's expected net per $360₮$ staked.
workedExamples.solution: $E(\text{net}) = 360 \cdot \frac{11}{36} - 360 \cdot \frac{25}{36} = 110 - 250 = -140₮$. Even-money odds on an $11$-in-$36$ event is a terrible trade — fair odds would pay $\frac{25}{11}$ times the stake.

commonMistakes.text: Judging a game by its best case.
commonMistakes.correction: "You can win 2000₮!" prices the dream, not the game. The game's worth is Σ payout·probability — 300₮ here. Compare the FEE to the EXPECTATION, never to the jackpot.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting the fee is paid on every play.
commonMistakes.correction: Expected NET folds in the certain cost: winnings minus fee. A game with positive expected winnings can still be a loser after the entry price.

tryIt.statement: Pay $200₮$; flip a coin; heads pays $500₮$, tails nothing. Fair, favorable, or unfavorable?
tryIt.solution: $E(\text{winnings}) = 250₮ > 200₮$ fee: FAVORABLE by $50₮$ per play. (Grab it — they're rare.)

tryIt.statement: A $1$-in-$20$ scratch card pays a single $3000₮$ prize. What's the fair price per card?

steps.title: The stall never loses
beats: Pay $500₮$, roll a die, win $100₮ \times$ roll.
Expected winnings: $100 \times 3.5 = 350₮$.
The stall keeps $150₮$ per play. Every play. Forever.
Fair would be a $350₮$ fee — fee $=$ expectation.

steps.title: Fair, favorable, edged
beats: Fair: $E(\text{net}) = 0$. Favorable: positive. House-edged: negative.
Edge as a rate: $\frac{150}{500} = 30\%$ of every fee, kept.
Casinos run $1$–$5\%$; lotteries $\approx 50\%$. Carnivals: ambitious.
Setting prizes: fair prize $= \frac{\text{fee}}{p}$.

steps.title: A game that balances at zero
steps.teach: Pay 1 to play; win 3 (net $+3$) with probability $\frac{1}{4}$, lose your stake (net $-1$) otherwise. Tap the bars: $(-1) \cdot \frac{3}{4} = -0.75$ and $3 \cdot \frac{1}{4} = +0.75$. They cancel — the triangle sits exactly at 0. That's what **fair** means: the long-run average gain is nothing, for either side.

steps.title: The fair fee
steps.prompt: A game pays $600₮$ with probability $\frac{1}{4}$ (else nothing). The fair entry fee is…
steps.explanation: $E(\text{winnings}) = 600 \times \frac{1}{4} = 150₮$ — that's the fair price. $600₮$ prices the jackpot; $300₮$ halves it for no reason the distribution knows.

steps.title: Rank the games
steps.prompt: Which game treats the player best? A: fee $100₮$, $E(\text{win}) = 95₮$. B: fee $1000₮$, $E(\text{win}) = 900₮$. C: fee $50₮$, $E(\text{win}) = 51₮$.
steps.explanation: Nets: A $-5₮$, B $-100₮$, C $+1₮$. Only C is favorable. Big expected winnings mean nothing next to a bigger fee — always compute the NET.

steps.title: Expected value is a long-run tool
steps.body: EV rules decisions you make MANY times (pricing, repeated bets, business offers). For one-shot high-stakes choices, the spread matters as much as the mean — next lesson builds exactly that number. A fair coin flip for your house is fair in EV and insane in variance.

steps.title: Roulette's edge is one green pocket
steps.body: European roulette pays 36-to-1 odds on a wheel with 37 pockets — the single green zero IS the house edge: expected net = −1/37 ≈ −2.7% of every bet. American wheels add a second zero, doubling the edge. Entire glass palaces are financed by one or two pockets of arithmetic.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Fair: fee = expected winnings; edge = the shortfall, kept forever.
Compare fee to expectation, never to the jackpot.
EV governs repeated decisions; one-shots also need the spread (next).

lessons.title: Variance & Standard Deviation
lessons.concreteComparison: Two investments both average $+100₮$ per month. Plan A: always exactly $+100₮$. Plan B: $+1100₮$ half the months, $-900₮$ the other half. Same expectation, wildly different lives. The number that separates them measures how far outcomes stray from the mean — the **variance**, and its square root, the **standard deviation**.
lessons.objective: Compute Var(X) = E[(X−μ)²] and SD, and interpret them as the distribution's risk/spread.
concept: **Variance** averages the SQUARED distance from the mean: $$\text{Var}(X) = \sum_x (x - \mu)^2 P(x), \quad \mu = E(X)$$ Squaring kills the sign (deviations don't cancel) and punishes big misses more than small ones.

**Standard deviation** $\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)}$ restores the original units — ₮, heads, points — making it the reportable number: "averages $100₮$, typically $\pm 1000₮$" tells the whole Plan-B story.
The computing shortcut: $\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$ — average of the squares minus square of the average. Same answer, less arithmetic (one pass for $E(X^2)$).

Interpretation: $\sigma = 0$ means certainty (Plan A); larger $\sigma$ means riskier/spreader. Two distributions with equal means are compared BY their $\sigma$ — expectation prices a game, deviation prices its drama.
lessons.keyIdea: Var(X) = Σ(x−μ)²P(x) = E(X²) − μ²; σ = √Var puts spread back in the variable's own units.

facts.title: Variance
facts.explanation: Mean squared deviation; the shortcut form needs one extra column, not a new table.

facts.title: Standard deviation
facts.explanation: Spread in the variable's own units — the honest companion to every mean.

workedExamples.statement: Plan A pays $+100$ always; Plan B pays $+1100$ or $-900$, each with probability $\frac{1}{2}$. Verify equal means, then compute each SD.
workedExamples.solution: Means: A is $100$; B: $\frac{1100 - 900}{2} = 100$ ✓. Var(A) $= 0$, so $\sigma_A = 0$. Var(B): deviations $\pm 1000$, squared $10^6$ each: $\text{Var} = 10^6$, $\sigma_B = 1000$. Same mean; B carries $1000$-unit typical swings.

workedExamples.statement: $X = $ heads in two coin flips. Compute Var$(X)$ and $\sigma$ via the shortcut.
workedExamples.solution: $\mu = 1$ (earlier). $E(X^2) = 0^2 \cdot \frac{1}{4} + 1^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} + 2^2 \cdot \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{2} + 1 = \frac{3}{2}$. Var $= \frac{3}{2} - 1^2 = \frac{1}{2}$; $\sigma = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.71$ heads.

workedExamples.statement: One fair die: compute Var$(X)$ with the shortcut, given $E(X) = \frac{7}{2}$.

commonMistakes.correction: Σ(x−μ)P(x) = 0 always — the mean is the balance point, deviations cancel by design. SQUARE first: that's the entire reason variance exists.

commonMistakes.text: Reporting variance in place of SD.
commonMistakes.correction: Var(B) = 1,000,000 'tugrik-squared' is unreadable; σ = 1000₮ speaks the game's language. Compute Var, REPORT σ.

tryIt.statement: $X$ takes values $0$ and $10$, each with probability $\frac{1}{2}$. Find $\mu$, Var, and $\sigma$.
Which is riskier: $Y$ paying $4$ or $6$ ($50$/$50$), or $Z$ paying $0$ or $10$ ($50$/$50$)? Compare $\sigma$'s.
tryIt.solution: Both means are $5$. $\sigma_Y = 1$; $\sigma_Z = 5$. $Z$ is five times spreadier — same price, five times the drama.

steps.title: Same average, different lives
beats: Plan A: $+100₮$ every month. Plan B: $+1100$ or $-900$, coin flip.
Both AVERAGE $+100₮$. Expectation can't tell them apart.
The missing number: how far outcomes STRAY from the mean.
Variance measures it. Standard deviation reports it.

steps.title: Square the misses, weigh, average
beats: $\text{Var}(X) = \sum (x - \mu)^2 P(x)$.
Squaring: kills sign cancellation, punishes big misses extra.
$\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}}$ — back to real units.
Shortcut: $\text{Var} = E(X^2) - \mu^2$. Learn both; use the shortcut.

steps.title: Same mean, measured spread
steps.teach: A uniform spinner on 2, 4, 6, 8 balances at $\mu = 5$ — and the whiskers reach one standard deviation each way, $\sigma = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$. The mean tells you where the distribution **sits**; $\sigma$ tells you how far a typical outcome strays from it.

steps.title: The cancellation trap
steps.explanation: The mean IS the balance point: weighted deviations cancel exactly, every time. That universal zero is why variance squares before averaging — otherwise every distribution would look riskless.

steps.title: Rank by risk
steps.prompt: Three $50/50$ games, all with mean $0$: A pays $\pm 1$; B pays $\pm 10$; C pays $\pm 100$. Their $\sigma$'s are…
steps.explanation: For symmetric $\pm a$ games, deviations are $\pm a$: Var $= a^2$, $\sigma = a$. (Option 2 lists the VARIANCES.) Same expectation, three risk levels — σ is the number that knows.

steps.title: Mean ± SD is the honest headline
steps.body: "Averages 100₮" is half a sentence. "Averages 100₮, typically ±1000₮" is a decision-ready one. Wherever you report an expectation — grades, delivery times, returns — attach the σ or expect to be misunderstood.

steps.title: Markowitz won a Nobel for mean-vs-σ
steps.body: Modern portfolio theory (Nobel 1990) formalizes exactly this lesson: every investment is a (mean, σ) pair, and rational investors trade expected return against standard deviation. The two-column table you just computed for coin flips runs trillion-dollar pension funds.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Var = Σ(x−μ)²P(x) = E(X²) − μ²; σ = √Var, in real units.
Raw deviations always cancel — squaring is the fix, not a habit.
Equal means are separated by σ: expectation prices, deviation warns.

lessons.title: Combining Expectations
lessons.concreteComparison: A taxi charges a $2000₮$ flag fee plus $1500₮$ per km, and your ride length averages $6$ km. Expected fare? You could build the fare's whole distribution... or just push the expectation through the formula: $E(\text{fare}) = 2000 + 1500 \times 6 = 11{,}000₮$. Expectation slides through additions and multiplications-by-constants untouched — the most laborsaving fact in the unit.
lessons.objective: Use linearity — E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b and E(X + Y) = E(X) + E(Y) — to compute expectations without rebuilding distributions.
concept: **Scaling and shifting:** $E(aX + b) = a\,E(X) + b$ — constants factor out, shifts pass through. (Spread behaves differently: adding $b$ leaves $\sigma$ alone, scaling multiplies it: $\sigma_{aX+b} = |a|\sigma_X$.)
**Addition:** $E(X + Y) = E(X) + E(Y)$ — ALWAYS, even when $X$ and $Y$ are dependent. This is linearity's superpower: no independence check needed for expectations of sums.
Consequence: expectations of COUNTS decompose. Two dice: $E(\text{sum}) = 3.5 + 3.5 = 7$ — no $36$-outcome table required. Ten dice: $35$, instantly.

The decomposition trick scales absurdly: the expected number of heads in $n$ flips is $n \times \frac{1}{2}$ because the count is a SUM of $n$ one-flip indicator variables, each with expectation $\frac{1}{2}$. (Unit 8 builds its main formula on exactly this.)
lessons.keyIdea: E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b and E(X+Y) = E(X) + E(Y) — the latter without ANY independence requirement; counts decompose into indicator sums.

facts.title: Linearity
facts.latex: E(aX + b) = a\,E(X) + b, \qquad E(X + Y) = E(X) + E(Y)
facts.explanation: Expectations pass through linear formulas; the sum rule holds even for dependent variables.

facts.title: Spread scales differently
facts.explanation: Shifts don't spread; scaling stretches deviations by |a|.

workedExamples.statement: Taxi fare: $2000₮$ flag plus $1500₮$/km; ride length $X$ has $E(X) = 6$ km. Find the expected fare.
workedExamples.solution: Fare $= 1500X + 2000$: $E = 1500 \times 6 + 2000 = 11{,}000₮$. No distribution needed — linearity carries the expectation through.

workedExamples.statement: Find the expected sum of two dice — then of ten dice.
workedExamples.solution: Each die: $E = 3.5$. Sum rule: two dice $\to 3.5 + 3.5 = 7$; ten dice $\to 10 \times 3.5 = 35$. (Compare: the ten-dice distribution has $6^{10} \approx 60$ million outcomes. Linearity skipped all of them.)

workedExamples.statement: A quiz has $8$ questions; a guesser is right on each with probability $\frac{1}{4}$. Find the expected number correct — via indicators.
workedExamples.solution: Write the count as $X = I_1 + \cdots + I_8$, where $I_k = 1$ if question $k$ is right. Each $E(I_k) = \frac{1}{4}$. Sum: $E(X) = 8 \times \frac{1}{4} = 2$. No binomial table, no cases — eight tiny expectations, added.

commonMistakes.correction: The sum rule needs NO independence — that's what makes it magic. (Variances DO care: Var(X+Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) only for independent X, Y.)
E(X²) ≠ [E(X)]² (their gap IS the variance!), and E(1/X) ≠ 1/E(X). Linearity covers aX + b and sums — nothing curvier.

tryIt.statement: A shop's morning revenue averages $80{,}000₮$ and afternoon revenue averages $120{,}000₮$ (they're strongly dependent — busy days are busy all day). Expected daily total?
tryIt.solution: Linearity doesn't care about the dependence: $E = 80{,}000 + 120{,}000 = 200{,}000₮$.

steps.title: Push the mean through the formula
beats: No fare distribution was ever built.
$E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b$ — expectation slides through linear math.

steps.title: Sums add — no permission needed
beats: $E(X + Y) = E(X) + E(Y)$. ALWAYS.
Dependent, independent, hostile — doesn't matter.
Two dice: $E(\text{sum}) = 7$ without the 36-cell table.
(Variance is the picky one — IT demands independence to add.)

steps.title: Through the formula
steps.explanation: $5 \times 20 + 30 = 130$. The $250$ multiplied the shift too; $100$ dropped it. Constants scale, shifts add — separately.

steps.title: Where linearity stops
steps.explanation: Only the LINEAR one: $2(3) + 1 = 7$. $E(X^2) = 9 + \text{Var}(X) \ge 9$ — the gap is literally the variance; and $E(1/X)$ obeys no such shortcut. Curves bend expectations.

steps.title: Count = sum of tiny 0/1 questions
steps.body: Any COUNT (heads, defects, matches, birthdays shared) is a sum of indicator variables — one per trial, each worth its own little probability. Expected count = sum of those probabilities. This one habit computes in one line what case-work computes in pages.

steps.title: The birthday-pairs one-liner
steps.body: Expected number of shared-birthday PAIRS in a class of 23: there are C(23,2) = 253 pairs, each matching with probability 1/365 — linearity gives 253/365 ≈ 0.69 expected pairs, in one line, despite the pairs being wildly dependent on each other. Linearity doesn't just save work; it makes impossible-looking questions polite.

steps.title: What you learned
points: E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b; σ scales by |a| and ignores shifts.
E(X + Y) = E(X) + E(Y) with NO independence requirement.
Counts decompose into indicators — expected count = sum of probabilities.

lessons.title: Capstone: Decisions Under Risk
lessons.concreteComparison: Insurance is a bet you HOPE to lose: you pay $120{,}000₮$ a year so that a $1\%$-chance $8{,}000{,}000₮$ disaster costs you nothing. The company's expected payout is $80{,}000₮$ — they pocket $40{,}000₮$ on average. Negative expected value... and possibly still the right choice. This capstone is where expectation, variance, and judgment meet.
lessons.objective: Analyze real decisions — insurance, warranties, lotteries, business choices — with expected value AND spread, and know when EV alone misleads.
concept: The EV workflow for any decision: list the outcomes of each option, attach probabilities and values, compute each option's $E$, compare. For REPEATED decisions, higher EV wins — the long run will collect it.

Insurance flips the sign sensibly: buyers accept negative EV to DELETE a catastrophic branch they cannot afford to experience even once. You're not buying average outcomes; you're buying a ceiling on the worst one. Variance reduction has real value that EV doesn't measure.

Same logic condemns most extended warranties and lotteries: negative EV AND no catastrophe being deleted (a broken blender is annoying, not ruinous; a lottery ticket deletes nothing). Negative EV is only justifiable when it buys survival or bounded downside.

The honest decision recipe: compute EV, compute (or eyeball) the spread, ask "can I afford the worst branch, and how many times will I make this choice?" Repeated + affordable → follow EV. One-shot + ruinous tail → pay to remove the tail.
lessons.keyIdea: Compute EV to price the choice; check the worst branch to see if EV is even the right judge — repeated & affordable follows EV, ruinous tails justify paying to delete them.

facts.title: The decision recipe
facts.explanation: Expectation compares; the tail and the repetition count decide whether expectation rules.

facts.title: Insurance logic
facts.explanation: Negative EV can be rational when it deletes an unaffordable branch.

workedExamples.statement: Insurance: premium $120{,}000₮$/year against a $1\%$-chance $8{,}000{,}000₮$ loss. Compute both sides' expected values, and say why a rational person might still buy.
workedExamples.solution: Company: collects $120{,}000$, expects to pay $0.01 \times 8{,}000{,}000 = 80{,}000$ — nets $+40{,}000₮$ per policy. Buyer: EV $= -40{,}000₮$. Rational anyway IF an $8$M₮ hit is unsurvivable: the premium converts a ruinous $1\%$ branch into a certain, affordable cost. EV prices the average; the buyer is pricing the worst case.

workedExamples.statement: A $25{,}000₮$ extended warranty covers a $2\%$-chance $300{,}000₮$ repair. Analyze it.
workedExamples.solution: Expected repair cost: $0.02 \times 300{,}000 = 6{,}000₮$ — the warranty charges $25{,}000₮$ for it: EV $= -19{,}000₮$, more than four times the fair price. And the deleted branch ($300$K) is unpleasant, not ruinous. Skip it; self-insure small stuff.

workedExamples.statement: A food stall chooses: location A nets $200{,}000₮$/day rain or shine; location B nets $500{,}000₮$ on dry days but $-100{,}000₮$ on rainy ones. With $P(\text{rain}) = 0.4$, compare for a season.
workedExamples.solution: $E_B = 0.6 \times 500{,}000 + 0.4 \times (-100{,}000) = 300{,}000 - 40{,}000 = 260{,}000₮ > E_A = 200{,}000₮$. Over a SEASON (repeated daily), B's edge compounds and rainy losses average out — take B if the stall can absorb losing days; take A if a bad week would sink the business. EV picks B; solvency has a vote.

commonMistakes.text: Applying long-run EV logic to a one-shot ruinous bet.
commonMistakes.correction: EV = long-run average. If the worst branch ends the game (bankruptcy, no retake), there IS no long run for averages to save you. Bound the tail first, optimize EV second.

Insurance logic covers CATASTROPHES only. Warranties on blenders and lottery tickets are negative EV with no ruin being prevented — pure edge donated. Same math, opposite verdicts, depending on the tail.

tryIt.statement: Bike insurance costs $30{,}000₮$/year; theft probability $5\%$; bike worth $400{,}000₮$. Fair price? Your call?
tryIt.solution: Fair: $0.05 \times 400{,}000 = 20{,}000₮$ — the premium runs $10{,}000₮$ over. If losing $400$K is absorbable, skip; if it's your only transport to work, the tail may be worth deleting.

tryIt.statement: Choose: certain $90{,}000₮$, or a $50\%$ shot at $200{,}000₮$. Compare EVs; when would you take the certain money anyway?
tryIt.solution: Gamble's EV: $100{,}000₮ > 90{,}000₮$. Repeated many times: gamble. One-shot with the money needed for rent: the certain $90$K's zero variance is worth the $10$K of EV.

steps.title: A bet you hope to lose
beats: Insurance: pay $120$K₮/year against a $1\%$, $8$M₮ disaster.
Company's expected payout: $80$K₮. They keep $40$K₮ on average.
Your EV is negative — and buying might STILL be right.
Expectation prices the average. Some decisions are about the worst case.

steps.title: EV, tail, repetition
beats: 1 — Compute each option's EV. (You can, now.)
2 — Stare at the worst branch: survivable?
3 — How many times will you make this choice?
Repeated + survivable → EV rules. One-shot + ruinous → cap the tail.

steps.title: Why insure the house but not the blender?
steps.prompt: Both contracts have negative EV. What separates them?
options: Only the house's loss branch is unaffordable — deleting IT is worth paying for
The blender warranty has better EV
steps.explanation: Insurance logic prices the TAIL, not the average: an 8M₮ house loss can end your finances; a 300K₮ repair stings and passes. Pay to delete ruin; self-insure inconvenience.

steps.title: Rent is due
steps.prompt: Certain $90$K₮ vs a $50\%$ shot at $200$K₮ (EV $100$K), exactly once, and you need $85$K₮ for rent tonight. Take…
options: The certain $90$K — the gamble's losing branch misses rent
The gamble — higher EV always wins
steps.explanation: The gamble's worst branch ($0$) is unsurvivable tonight; there's no long run in a one-shot. EV picks the gamble ONLY when you can afford to lose and repeat. Constraints outrank averages.

steps.title: You now out-reason the carnival
steps.body: Unit 7's kit: distributions describe, expectation prices, variance warns, linearity computes, and the tail-test decides. Next unit: the single most useful distribution in the world — repeated yes/no trials — where Act One's chooses and Act Two's independence finally shake hands.

steps.title: The St. Petersburg lesson
steps.body: A 1713 puzzle offers a coin game with INFINITE expected value — yet nobody sane pays more than a few coins to play. Daniel Bernoulli's resolution founded utility theory: money's value bends (the second million matters less than the first), so raw EV can't be the whole story of rational choice. Your rent-night instinct has a 300-year-old theorem behind it.

steps.title: What you learned — Unit 7 complete
points: EV compares options; the worst branch decides if EV is the judge.
Pay to delete ruin (insurance); never pay to delete inconvenience (warranties, lotteries).
Next: the binomial — counting successes across repeated trials.

lessons.title: The Distribution Function
lessons.concreteComparison: A distribution table answers "what's the chance X **equals** 2?" The distribution function answers the accumulating question: "what's the chance X is **at most** 2?" It's the running total of the table — like a savings balance versus individual deposits. And once you hold running totals, any interval is one subtraction: $P(2 < X \le 5) = F(5) - F(2)$.
lessons.objective: Read and build F(x) = P(X ≤ x), convert between a distribution table and its running totals, and extract interval probabilities via F(b) − F(a).
concept: The **distribution function** (also called the CDF) of a random variable is $F(x) = P(X \le x)$ — the probability accumulated up to and including $x$. It starts at $0$ far to the left, never decreases, and ends at $1$: all the probability, banked.

For a discrete $X$, $F$ is a **staircase**: flat between the values, jumping at each value $x$ by exactly $P(X = x)$. Table → $F$ is running totals; $F$ → table is reading the jump sizes. The two carry identical information.

The payoff formula: $$P(a < X \le b) = F(b) - F(a).$$ Everything at most $b$, minus everything at most $a$, leaves exactly the interval $(a, b]$. Mind the endpoints: the left end is excluded, the right end included — that's what the $\le$ inside $F$ dictates.

Exams also hand you $F$ as a formula — e.g. $F(x) = \frac{x}{4}$ on $0 < x \le 4$ (a uniform ramp from $0$ to $1$). Nothing changes: interval probability is still $F(b) - F(a)$, now computed by plugging into the formula.
lessons.keyIdea: F(x) = P(X ≤ x) is the running total of probability; any interval costs one subtraction: P(a < X ≤ b) = F(b) − F(a).

facts.title: Definition
facts.explanation: The probability accumulated up to and including x — from 0 on the far left to 1 on the far right.

facts.title: Interval rule
facts.explanation: Running total at b minus running total at a — endpoints follow the ≤ inside F.

facts.title: Jump = point probability
facts.explanation: For discrete X the staircase jumps at each value by exactly that value's probability.

workedExamples.statement: $X$ takes the values $1, 2, 3$ with probabilities $\frac{1}{4}, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{4}$. Build the distribution function and read off $P(X \le 2)$.
A random variable has distribution function $F(x) = 0$ for $x \le 0$, $F(x) = \frac{x}{4}$ for $0 < x \le 4$, and $F(x) = 1$ for $x > 4$. Find $P(3 < X \le 4)$.
With $F(x) = \frac{x}{3}$ on $0 < x \le 3$ (and $0$ before, $1$ after), find $P(1 < X \le 3)$.

commonMistakes.correction: F accumulates: F(2) is the chance of AT MOST 2, not exactly 2. Exactly-2 is the JUMP of the staircase at 2 (discrete) — read it as F(2) minus the level just before.

commonMistakes.text: Wrong endpoint bookkeeping in F(b) − F(a).
commonMistakes.correction: F(b) − F(a) covers a < X ≤ b — left end OUT, right end IN. For discrete X, P(a ≤ X ≤ b) needs the jump at a added back.

tryIt.statement: A distribution function satisfies $F(2) = 0.7$ and $F(5) = 0.9$. Find $P(2 < X \le 5)$.
$X$ is uniform on $[0, 8]$, so $F(x) = \frac{x}{8}$ there. Find $P(X > 6)$.

steps.title: Tables answer one question at a time
steps.body: A distribution table hands you $P(X = x)$ value by value. But most real questions are about RANGES: at most $2$, more than $6$, between $3$ and $5$. Adding table entries every time gets old. There's a smarter object: bank the probability as you go.

steps.title: F(x) — probability, accumulated
steps.body: Define $F(x) = P(X \le x)$: the running total up to $x$. It climbs from $0$ to $1$ and never dips. For a table $\{1{:}\,\frac14,\; 2{:}\,\frac12,\; 3{:}\,\frac14\}$ the totals are $\frac14, \frac34, 1$ — a staircase whose JUMPS are the original probabilities. Table and staircase are the same information, worn two ways.
beats: F(x) = P(X ≤ x), the running total
climbs 0 → 1, never decreases
discrete: staircase, jump at x equals P(X = x)

steps.title: Read the staircase
steps.prompt: For the table $\{1{:}\,\frac{1}{4},\; 2{:}\,\frac{1}{2},\; 3{:}\,\frac{1}{4}\}$, how big is the jump of $F$ at $x = 2$?
steps.explanation: The staircase jumps at each value by that value's probability: $P(X = 2) = \frac{1}{2}$. ($F(2) = \frac{3}{4}$ is the LEVEL after the jump, not the jump.)

steps.title: One subtraction
steps.prompt: A distribution function has $F(1) = 0.3$ and $F(4) = 0.8$. What is $P(1 < X \le 4)$?
steps.explanation: $P(1 < X \le 4) = F(4) - F(1) = 0.8 - 0.3 = 0.5$. Never add the two — $F$ values are totals, not pieces.

steps.title: Endpoints follow the ≤
steps.body: $F(b) - F(a)$ delivers $a < X \le b$: left end excluded, right end included. On continuous ramps single points carry zero probability so it rarely matters — but on discrete staircases the jump at $a$ is real. Check which world the problem lives in before shrugging at endpoints.

steps.title: Why statisticians love F
steps.body: Tables only exist for discrete variables, but EVERY random variable — discrete, continuous, or mixed — has a distribution function. It's the universal passport of probability theory: one object that describes them all.

steps.title: What sticks
points: $F(x) = P(X \le x)$: the running total, climbing $0 \to 1$.
Discrete → staircase; the jump at $x$ is $P(X = x)$.
$P(a < X \le b) = F(b) - F(a)$ — one subtraction, endpoints per the $\le$.

practice.statement: One die is rolled; $X = 1$ if the roll is even, else $0$. Build the distribution and find $E(X)$.
A raffle sells $250$ tickets at $400₮$; prizes are one $40{,}000₮$ and two $10{,}000₮$. Find the expected net per ticket.
What entry fee makes this game fair: draw one card, win $2600₮$ if it's a heart?
$X$ pays $\pm 4$ with equal probability. Find Var$(X)$ and $\sigma$.
Five dice are rolled. Find the expected total, and the expected number of sixes.
Phone insurance: $18{,}000₮$/year, covers a $6\%$-chance $250{,}000₮$ replacement. Find the fair premium and the company's expected margin.
A bag holds $2$ red and $3$ white balls. Two balls are drawn without replacement; let $X$ be the number of white balls drawn. Build the distribution of $X$ and find $E[X]$.
For the same draw ($2$ red, $3$ white, draw $2$; $X$ = white count), find $\mathrm{Var}(X)$.

testYourself.statement: Three coins are flipped; $X = $ number of heads. Build the full distribution, then find $E(X)$ and Var$(X)$.
A game: pay $300₮$, draw one card. Aces pay $2000₮$, other face cards (J, Q, K) pay $500₮$, anything else pays nothing. Fair, favorable, or edged — by how much?
testYourself.solution: $E(\text{win}) = 2000 \cdot \frac{4}{52} + 500 \cdot \frac{12}{52} = \frac{8000 + 6000}{52} = \frac{14000}{52} \approx 269₮$. Net $\approx -31₮$: house-edged by about $10\%$ of the fee.

testYourself.statement: Games $Y$ ($\pm 2$, 50/50) and $Z$ ($\pm 20$, 50/50) both cost nothing. Compute both $\sigma$'s, and say which a rent-constrained player prefers and why.
testYourself.solution: Both means $0$; $\sigma_Y = 2$, $\sigma_Z = 20$. Equal EV — the constrained player takes $Y$: identical average, one-tenth the swing; the worst branch is survivable.

testYourself.statement: A delivery service charges $3000₮$ base plus $800₮$/km. Distances average $7$ km. Find the expected charge, and what happens to it if every distance grows by exactly $2$ km.
testYourself.solution: $E = 800 \times 7 + 3000 = 8600₮$. Distances $+2$ km: $E(\text{new}) = 800 \times 9 + 3000 = 10{,}200₮$ — linearity shifts the mean by $800 \times 2 = 1600₮$, no distribution rebuilt.

testYourself.statement: A $20$-question multiple-choice test has $4$ options per question. Using indicators, find the expected score of a pure guesser, and the expected score under "$+1$ right, $-\frac{1}{3}$ wrong" scoring.
testYourself.solution: Right-count: $E = 20 \times \frac{1}{4} = 5$. Penalty scoring per question: $\frac{1}{4}(1) + \frac{3}{4}(-\frac{1}{3}) = \frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{4} = 0$ — total expected score $0$: the classic penalty exactly cancels blind guessing.

testYourself.statement: Crop insurance costs $500{,}000₮$; drought probability $8\%$ would cost the farm $5{,}000{,}000₮$. Compute the fair premium, the EV of buying, and give the two-sentence recommendation a good advisor would.
testYourself.solution: Fair: $0.08 \times 5M = 400{,}000₮$; buying has EV $-100{,}000₮$. Recommendation: the premium runs $25\%$ over fair — but if a $5$M₮ loss would end the farm, buy anyway: you're deleting an unsurvivable branch, and that's what insurance is FOR. If the farm holds reserves that could absorb it, self-insure and bank the edge.

testYourself.statement: A distribution function satisfies $F(x) = 0$ for $x \le 0$, $F(x) = \frac{x}{5}$ for $0 < x \le 5$, $F(x) = 1$ after. Find $P(2 < X \le 4)$.
